A correction shares and references the original event's natural key; a new event has its own. CDC updates become reversal+restate pairs; CDC inserts become new rows. Misclassifying this either double-counts or loses corrections.

q: A dimension has a ragged, variable-depth hierarchy (org chart, bill-of-materials). How do you model it for roll-up queries?
a: A self-referential parent_key handles a fixed shallow hierarchy but forces recursive CTEs and cannot roll a fact up to every ancestor in one join. The robust pattern is a hierarchy bridge table that materializes every ancestor-descendant pair, storing parent_key, child_key, depth_from_parent, and flags for the top and bottom of the path. A fact joins through the bridge so a single query can aggregate a measure to any level of the org or BOM without recursion. The cost is that the bridge must be rebuilt when the hierarchy changes and you must weight or filter carefully to avoid double-counting shared sub-trees.
fj: Just add a parent_id column and recurse — that covers any hierarchy.
fs: A parent_id self-join needs a recursive CTE and can't roll up to all ancestors cleanly. A hierarchy bridge materializing every ancestor-descendant pair lets one join aggregate to any level of a ragged hierarchy.

code.label: hierarchy bridge roll-up
lines: FROM fct_cost f JOIN bridge_org b

followups.q: How do you prevent double-counting when a node appears under multiple parents (a true graph, not a tree)?
followups.a: Add an allocation weight to each bridge row so a shared sub-tree's measure is split across its parents (e.g. 0.5/0.5), and multiply the measure by weight before summing. For strict reporting you may instead pick a primary parent and flag secondary paths so default rollups follow one tree. Without weighting, a node reachable via two ancestors contributes its full measure to both, inflating totals.

followups.q: What has to happen to the bridge table when someone reorganizes the org chart?
followups.a: You rebuild the affected ancestor-descendant pairs, because moving one node changes every path through it. Many teams version the bridge (SCD2-style effective dating) so historical facts roll up under the structure in effect at event time, not today's. The bridge's maintenance cost — full or partial rebuild on every reorg — is the main trade-off versus a simple parent_id.

followups.q: When is a recursive CTE on parent_id actually the better choice over a bridge table?
followups.a: When the hierarchy is shallow, small, changes often, and you don't need to roll a fact up to all ancestors in one join. The recursive CTE avoids maintaining a materialized bridge and is fine for ad-hoc traversal of a few thousand nodes. The bridge wins when the hierarchy is deep/ragged, queried constantly, and rollup-to-any-level performance matters more than avoiding the rebuild cost.

q: Explain SCD Type 7: how does it give both current and historical dimension views, and how does it differ from Type 6?
a: Type 7 keeps a pure Type 2 dimension (versioned rows with effective dating) but has the fact carry two foreign keys to it: the durable/natural key and the surrogate version key. Joining on the surrogate key gives the as-was historical attribute in effect at event time; joining on the durable key against the current row (is_current = true) gives the as-is current attribute — all from the same Type 2 table with no extra columns. The contrast with Type 6 is where the dual view comes from: Type 6 adds an overwritten 'current' column onto each Type 2 row, while Type 7 adds a second key to the fact and resolves current-vs-historical purely by which key you join on. Type 7 keeps the dimension clean; Type 6 keeps the fact narrower.
fj: Type 7 is identical to Type 6 — both just give you current and historical values.
fs: Type 6 adds an overwritten current column to each Type 2 row. Type 7 instead puts two keys (durable + surrogate) on the fact: join on surrogate for as-was, on durable + is_current for as-is.

code.label: two keys on the fact
lines: -- as-was: surrogate key at event time

followups.q: What is the 'durable key' in Type 7 and how does it differ from the natural key?
followups.a: The durable (or persistent) key is a stable warehouse-assigned identifier for the entity that stays constant across all its SCD2 versions — unlike the version surrogate key, which is unique per row. It often equals the natural key but is decoupled so it survives source rekeys. The fact carries both: the version surrogate (for as-was) and the durable key (for as-is via the current row).

followups.q: What's the practical reason to choose Type 7 over Type 6?
followups.a: Write cost. Type 6 must overwrite the `current` column on every historical row when an attribute changes (write amplification); Type 7 just resolves current at query time by joining on the durable key to `is_current = TRUE`, so a change is a plain Type 2 insert with no historical-row updates. For high-churn dimensions Type 7 is cheaper to maintain at the cost of one extra fact key and a join.

followups.q: Does Type 7 require storing two keys on the fact, and is that a meaningful cost?
followups.a: Yes — the fact carries both the version surrogate and the durable key. On a columnar store the extra key column compresses well and the cost is negligible; on very wide high-volume facts it's a small add. The benefit — clean as-was vs as-is from one Type 2 dimension with no overwritten columns — usually outweighs it. It's the standard modern approach to dual-perspective reporting.

q: How should a surrogate date key be designed, and is a 'smart' integer like 20260605 a good idea?
a: The date dimension is the one place a meaningful smart integer key is broadly endorsed: a YYYYMMDD integer such as 20260605 is compact, naturally sortable, human-readable in raw fact rows, and lets you partition fact tables on the key directly. This is a deliberate exception to the rule that surrogate keys carry no business meaning, justified because the calendar never rekeys and the encoding is stable forever. Crucially, you still reserve a special key for unknown or not-applicable dates — often -1 or a sentinel like 19000101 — so late-arriving or null dates have a real row to join to and never produce NULL foreign keys. For every other dimension, prefer a meaningless sequence or hash surrogate.
fj: Smart keys are always bad, so the date key should be a meaningless sequence too.
fs: The date dimension is the accepted exception: a YYYYMMDD integer is sortable, readable, and partition-friendly, and the calendar never rekeys. But still reserve a sentinel key for unknown/NA dates.

code.label: smart date key + unknown row
lines: -- late/NULL dates join to -1, not NULL

followups.q: Why -1 or 19000101 for the unknown member instead of just NULL?
followups.a: A real sentinel row lets the fact keep a non-null FK and use inner joins without silently dropping rows — NULL FKs break inner joins and force LEFT JOINs everywhere. The sentinel also gives analysts something to filter on ('orders with Unknown ship date'). 19000101 sorts before any real date and won't collide with a genuine entry; -1 works where the key is a plain surrogate.

followups.q: Does the YYYYMMDD smart-key exception apply to a time-of-day dimension too?
followups.a: Yes, by the same logic — a `dim_time` can use an HHMMSS or minute-of-day integer that's sortable and readable, because the clock never rekeys. The exception holds wherever the encoded domain is fixed and stable forever (calendars, clocks). For any business entity that can rekey or version, you still use a meaningless sequence or hash so the key carries no fragile business meaning.

followups.q: What goes wrong if you partition a fact table on a YYYYMMDD smart date key?
followups.a: Usually nothing — it's actually a benefit: the integer is monotonic and aligns with date ranges, so range-partitioning and partition pruning on `date_key BETWEEN 20260101 AND 20260131` work cleanly. The pitfall is the reserved sentinel rows (-1, 19000101): they land in an out-of-range or first partition and can skew partition sizes, so account for them when you define partition boundaries.

q: A dimension has grown to 200+ columns mixing stable and rapidly changing attributes. How do you decide whether to split it?
a: First check whether the width is actually a problem: on a columnar warehouse a wide dimension is often fine because unused columns are pruned, so don't split reflexively. Split when distinct concerns collide — most commonly when rapidly changing, low-cardinality attributes force SCD Type 2 versioning that explodes the row count; carve those into a mini-dimension (Type 4) the fact references via a second key. Also split when groups of columns have different ownership, security, or refresh cadence, or when a sub-group is reused by other facts and deserves to be a conformed dimension. The anti-pattern is splitting on width alone, which just reintroduces snowflake joins for no benefit.
fj: A 200-column dimension is obviously too wide — split it up to be safe.
fs: On columnar storage, width alone isn't a reason to split — unused columns are pruned. Split when rapidly-changing attributes force SCD2 row explosion (use a mini-dimension) or when groups differ in ownership/security/cadence.

followups.q: How do you identify WHICH columns are causing the SCD2 row explosion?
followups.a: Profile change frequency per column: for each attribute, count how often its value changes per entity over a window (distinct values per customer per year). The handful of high-churn, low-cardinality columns driving most version inserts are your split candidates for a mini-dimension. Stable columns (signup date, country) aren't the problem and stay in the main dimension.

followups.q: Splitting for security or ownership — what's the modeling pattern there?
followups.a: Carve the sensitive or differently-owned columns into their own dimension keyed back to the main one (a one-to-one outrigger), so you can apply distinct row/column-level security, masking, or refresh cadence to that slice. PII split out can be governed separately from the descriptive bulk. The fact still references the main dimension; the sensitive sub-dimension joins one-to-one.

followups.q: Could you keep the 200 columns but use a semantic/view layer instead of physically splitting?
followups.a: Yes — often the better first move. If width itself isn't hurting (columnar pruning handles it), expose curated views that surface only the relevant subset per consumer, leaving one physical table. Only physically split for a concrete forcing function: SCD2 row explosion, divergent refresh cadence, or security boundaries. Otherwise the view layer wins without the join cost.

q: How do you model Salesforce (CRM) source data into a dimensional warehouse — what are the trickiest objects and why?
a: Salesforce exposes Accounts, Contacts, Opportunities, Activities, and many lookup-joined junction objects. The trickiest objects are Opportunities (complex stage history in OpportunityHistory requiring SCD Type 2 or an accumulating snapshot for pipeline reporting), polymorphic lookup fields like WhatId/WhoId that resolve to different object types at runtime (model as a junk dimension flag plus separate FK columns for each possible type), and formula/rollup fields that recalculate silently without a change timestamp, defeating hash-based change detection. Standard patterns: load raw Salesforce via Fivetran/Airbyte into a bronze layer exactly as-is, conform Account→dim_account and Contact→dim_contact with proper surrogate keys in silver, and build fct_opportunity_pipeline as an accumulating snapshot with stage-date columns for won/lost/closed dates. Never trust Salesforce's SystemModstamp alone for CDC — custom trigger fields or Bulk API 2.0 change-capture streams are more reliable.
fj: I'd just sync Salesforce tables directly and query them as the source of truth.
fs: Raw Salesforce tables have polymorphic FKs, formula fields that change silently, and audit-unfriendly timestamps. You need a bronze landing, silver conformance with surrogate keys, and dimensional marts — particularly an accumulating snapshot for opportunity pipeline.

code.label: opportunity accumulating snapshot
lines: -- = sales cycle length, one row per opp

followups.q: How do you handle Salesforce custom objects and fields in a dimensional model?
followups.a: Custom objects map to new dimension or fact tables depending on their grain and whether they carry measures. Custom fields on standard objects are additional attributes on existing dimensions — add them as columns if stable or as a separate extension satellite (Data Vault style) if they change at a different cadence. Use the Salesforce Metadata API to detect schema drift: a new custom field on Account should trigger an automated schema migration alert in your pipeline, not silently drop data.

followups.q: Salesforce OpportunityHistory has one row per stage change — how do you turn it into a usable fact?
followups.a: Two options: (1) accumulating snapshot — PIVOT or window-function the stage history into one row per opportunity with stage-date columns; use FIRST_VALUE ordered by CreatedDate per stage to get the first entry date. (2) transaction fact — keep one row per stage transition, which makes frequency and drop-off analysis easy but requires a self-join or pivot to compute stage-to-stage durations. Use both: the accumulating snapshot for cycle-time reporting, the transaction fact for funnel drop-off analysis.

followups.q: How do you resolve a Salesforce Account that has been merged (duplicate merge operation) in the dimensional model?
followups.a: Salesforce marks the loser account with `IsDeleted=true` and sets `MasterRecordId` on it pointing to the winner. At silver/dimension build time: retire the loser's surrogate key by closing its Type 2 row, map the winner's surrogate key as the current active row, and issue a fact backfill to re-stamp any historical facts that referenced the loser surrogate with the winner surrogate — or create a merge-resolution bridge. Document the merge event in a `dim_account_merge_audit` table for compliance.

q: How do you model SAP ERP source data (SD, MM, FI modules) into a dimensional warehouse — what are the structural differences from modeling CRM data?
a: SAP is normalized to 3NF across hundreds of tables per module: Sales & Distribution (SD) spreads one sales order across VBAK (header), VBAP (items), VBEP (schedule lines), and VBFA (document flow); Financial Accounting (FI) stores double-entry postings in BKPF (header) and BSEG (line items) with GL account and cost-center hierarchies in separate master data tables. The key difference from CRM is that SAP data is already relational and normalized — the challenge is not denormalization but assembly: reconstructing business-meaningful facts from many source tables, handling SAP's compound keys (MANDT + VBELN + POSNR), and mapping SAP's coded fields (order type, rejection reason) from text tables (TVAK, T024) that function as dimension lookup tables. At silver, join and assemble the relevant SAP tables into business-entity grains; at gold, build Kimball star facts for SD order lines, FI journal entries, and MM goods movements.
fj: I'd replicate the SAP tables as-is into the warehouse and query them directly.
fs: SAP's 3NF structure spreads one order across 4+ tables with compound keys and coded fields that need text-table joins. The warehouse must assemble business-entity grains in silver, then build dimensional star marts in gold — not replicate the SAP relational model.

followups.q: How do you handle SAP's MANDT (client) compound key in a multi-client SAP system?
followups.a: Include MANDT as a source system attribute on your surrogate key hash (hash_key = md5(mandt || vbeln || posnr)) so records from different clients never collide. Store MANDT as a natural key column in the dimension and as a source-system partition in silver. In most single-tenant setups MANDT is always '100' and can be filtered out early; in multi-client stacks it becomes part of entity resolution when data from multiple SAP clients feeds one warehouse.

followups.q: SAP FI has debit and credit postings in BSEG — how do you model the GL journal entry fact?
followups.a: One row per BSEG line item is the grain (one journal entry line per account per document). Store the signed posting amount (positive for debits, negative for credits) so you can SUM across both sides and the result should net to zero per balanced document. GL account maps to a GL dimension; cost center, profit center, and WBS element are additional dimension FKs from the controlling module. The document header (BKPF) fields like posting date and document type live in the fact or as a degenerate dimension (document_number), not in a separate header fact, because most FI questions are answered at the line level.

followups.q: How do you reconcile SAP SD revenue against SAP FI accounting revenue in the warehouse?
followups.a: SD and FI can show different numbers due to billing timing, revenue recognition rules, and inter-company postings. Model both as separate facts — fct_sd_order_lines (statistical order revenue) and fct_fi_journal_entries (recognized revenue) — with a shared conformed dim_date and dim_customer. Build a reconciliation mart that joins them on billing document number (a degenerate dim in FI that links to the SD billing document key) to surface differences. The reconciliation fact is itself a conformed, auditable mart, not an ad-hoc query.

q: What are Point-in-Time (PIT) tables and Reference Tables in Data Vault 2.0, and how do they bridge the vault to Kimball-style consumption?
a: A Point-in-Time (PIT) table is a pre-computed lookup helper: for each hub entity and each snapshot timestamp, it stores the `load_ts` of the most recent satellite row active as of that timestamp for every satellite hanging off that hub. Instead of N range-join lookups at query time (one per satellite), a query joins the hub to the PIT table once and gets all satellite pointers, then joins each satellite on its exact `load_ts` — O(1) lookups rather than O(N) range scans. A Reference Table (REF) stores static or slowly changing code-list data (currency codes, country codes, status codes) that is shared across hubs and links but doesn't fit the hub-link-satellite pattern because it has no business key of its own from a transactional source; it is a flat lookup keyed by its own code. Together, PITs enable fast satellite querying and REFs supply dimensional decode context, both feeding the Business Vault or Kimball gold mart layer on top.
fj: Data Vault is just hubs, links, and satellites — there's nothing else in the pattern.
fs: Data Vault 2.0 also defines PIT tables to make satellite range-lookups O(1), Reference Tables for code-list data, and the Business Vault for derived/rule-applied data — all supporting a Kimball mart on top.

code.label: PIT table structure
lines: -- on exact load_ts: no range scan

followups.q: How do you rebuild a PIT table when a new satellite is added to the vault?
followups.a: Add the new satellite's `load_ts` column to the PIT schema and regenerate the PIT from the earliest snapshot timestamp forward — effectively a full rebuild. In practice, PIT tables are regenerated on a schedule (daily or per pipeline run) from the satellite history, so adding a column is a schema migration + one full refresh cycle. The PIT is a derived structure, not a source of truth, so rebuilding it is safe.

followups.q: What is the Business Vault in Data Vault 2.0?
followups.a: The Business Vault is a layer within the vault (separate from raw vault) that stores computed, rule-applied, or derived data still in vault structures — for example a computed `credit_score_band` derived from a raw score satellite, or a `resolved_entity_hk` from a deduplication rule. It keeps business logic in one place rather than recomputing in every mart. The Business Vault feeds Kimball gold marts with already-rule-applied data, so the mart SQL stays thin.

followups.q: When is a Data Vault PIT table NOT worth the complexity?
followups.a: When each hub has only one or two satellites. The PIT's value is eliminating multiple range joins — with one satellite, the join is already simple. PIT tables pay off when hubs have 4+ satellites queried together, when the satellite history is deep (millions of load-ts rows), or when mart query performance is unacceptable without them. For small vaults or teams below a certain scale, PIT overhead outweighs the gain; query the satellites directly with range joins.

q: Explain SCD Type 3 — when is it genuinely useful, what are its hard limits, and why do most warehouses use Type 2 instead?
a: SCD Type 3 adds a `previous_value` column alongside the `current_value` column — no new rows, just one extra column per tracked attribute. It is genuinely useful in a narrow scenario: a one-time, planned corporate reorganization where you must report both before and after the change for exactly two states (pre-reorg and post-reorg region assignments) and want to avoid dimension growth. Its hard limits are that it only ever tracks one prior state (the column can't be extended to track three historical states without schema changes), it cannot support arbitrary point-in-time queries against an unknown number of past states, and it conflates the change date with the load timestamp so you lose accurate effective dating. Most warehouses use Type 2 instead because Type 2 handles arbitrary history depth, supports precise temporal queries, and costs only dimension growth — while Type 3 saves row count at the expense of query expressiveness and hard-coded history depth.
fj: SCD Type 3 is a more efficient alternative to Type 2 because it doesn't create extra rows.
fs: Type 3 is only appropriate for a one-time planned change where exactly two states matter. It cannot support arbitrary history depth or point-in-time queries — those require Type 2 versioned rows.

followups.q: Could you extend Type 3 to track three prior states with three extra columns? Why don't teams do this?
followups.a: Technically yes — add `prev_region_2`, `prev_region_3`. But every additional change requires another schema migration (ALTER TABLE) to add a column, and queries must check N columns to reconstruct history order. At two or three changes you've effectively built a Type 3 extension that is harder to query and maintain than Type 2 versioned rows, which scale to arbitrary depth without schema changes. This is why nobody extends Type 3 beyond one prior column in practice.

followups.q: When a Type 3 reorg change is applied, do historical facts automatically report the new region or the old?
followups.a: Historical facts still join to the dimension by surrogate key. After a Type 3 UPDATE, the current_region column now reflects the new value, so any join on that surrogate key returns the new region — even for facts that occurred before the reorg. That's a deliberate Type 3 feature for the 'both periods' use case, but it means you can't distinguish 'what region was this customer in when the sale happened' from 'what region are they in now' purely from the dimension — you need the `prev_region` + the known reorg date.

followups.q: A company does a one-time territory realignment. Would you use Type 3 or Type 2 for the territory attribute?
followups.a: Type 3 is actually justified here — one planned change, exactly two states (before and after), and you want to report historical revenue by both the old and new territory assignments without a schema rewrite. If there's any chance of a second realignment within the data retention window, choose Type 2 immediately — the one-time assumption is the condition Type 3 depends on, and violations are expensive to retrofit.

q: You are building a gold-layer mart that combines CRM (Salesforce) and ERP (SAP) data. How do you create conformed dimensions and what are the entity-resolution challenges?
a: Conformed dimensions require that dim_customer, dim_product, and dim_date mean exactly the same thing — identical surrogate keys, attribute names, rollup hierarchies — when used by both fct_crm_opportunity and fct_erp_order_line. The entity-resolution challenge is that Salesforce uses Account ID (Salesforce GUID) and SAP uses Customer Number (KUNNR) for the same real-world company; the silver integration layer must match and resolve these to a single enterprise customer_key using deterministic rules (exact match on tax ID, fuzzy match on company name+address, or a golden-record MDM feed). Once resolved, the conformed dim_customer carries both source IDs (crm_account_id, erp_kunnr) alongside one warehouse surrogate. The same pattern applies to dim_product (Salesforce Product ID vs SAP Material Number). Without this resolution, 'revenue by customer' adds up differently depending on whether you query the CRM or ERP fact, and drill-across breaks silently.
fj: I'd just join the Salesforce and SAP tables on customer name — they should match.
fs: Name matching is unreliable — companies have aliases, abbreviations, and spelling variants. Silver needs deterministic entity resolution using stable identifiers (tax ID, D-U-N-S number) or an MDM golden record, producing one warehouse surrogate that both CRM and ERP facts reference.

followups.q: How do you handle a customer that exists in Salesforce but not yet in SAP (a prospect)?
followups.a: The conformed dim_customer includes them with a NULL erp_kunnr natural key — the SAP ID is optional at the dimension level. CRM facts reference this customer normally. ERP facts can't reference them until they convert to a paying customer in SAP; the link between the two facts will be missing until conversion. Track this in the dimension with an `is_customer_in_erp` flag so analysts know which rows have cross-system history.

followups.q: What is the Kimball bus matrix role in planning a CRM + ERP integration?
followups.a: The bus matrix is the planning artifact: rows are business processes (Salesforce opportunity pipeline, SAP sales orders, SAP FI billing), columns are conformed dimensions (dim_customer, dim_product, dim_date, dim_sales_org). An X marks which dimensions each process uses. Where both Salesforce and SAP share dim_customer and dim_date, drill-across is valid. Where they don't share a conformed dimension, cross-process comparison is not defensible. Build the bus matrix before coding and use it to scope the entity-resolution work.

followups.q: How do you handle the same transaction appearing in both Salesforce (booked opportunity) and SAP (sales order) without double-counting revenue?
followups.a: Define the canonical revenue fact per business process: CRM = pipeline/booked value at opportunity close; ERP = recognized billing revenue from invoice. These are different business measures, not duplicates — a Salesforce won opportunity is not the same row as an SAP billing document. Expose them as separate facts in the mart (fct_opportunity and fct_billing) and build a reconciliation mart joining them on the shared invoice or PO number (a degenerate dimension). Explicitly document which fact is the system-of-record for each revenue definition.

q: What is the Medallion Architecture's Bronze → Silver → Gold pattern, and what concrete transformations happen at each layer boundary?
a: Bronze is the raw landing zone: data is ingested as-is from source systems (Salesforce, SAP, Kafka, S3 files) with no transformation — only the source format plus ingestion metadata (load timestamp, source file name, pipeline run ID). Silver is the conformance and integration layer: raw records are cleaned (null handling, type casting), deduplicated (Kafka exactly-once or CDC dedup), conformed to standard schemas (field renames, unit normalization), entity-resolved across sources (one customer_key for Salesforce + SAP), and history-tracked (SCD Type 2 dimensions or Data Vault satellite appends). Gold is the consumption layer: business-friendly dimensional star schemas or OBT marts, metric-level aggregates, and governed definitions of KPIs (revenue, churn, conversion) ready for BI tools, ML feature stores, or API serving. The boundary rules: Bronze → Silver = make it trustworthy; Silver → Gold = make it useful. Bronze is immutable (append-only); Silver may be idempotent upserts; Gold is materialized and often rebuilt or incrementally maintained.
fj: Medallion is just three folders: raw, clean, and final — same data, different names.
fs: Each layer has a distinct transformation contract: Bronze is raw+auditable, Silver is trusted+conformed+integrated, Gold is business-ready with declared metrics. The boundary transforms — conformance, entity resolution, SCD tracking in silver; dimensional modeling and metric governance in gold — are the actual engineering work.

followups.q: Should Bronze ever be queryable by BI tools?
followups.a: Almost never — Bronze is raw, untyped, and potentially contains PII, duplicates, and partial records. It exists for pipeline reproducibility and debugging, not consumption. Rarely, an SLA-sensitive use case queries Bronze directly before Silver is ready (e.g. real-time fraud detection), but this requires explicit governance: schema contracts, masked PII, and documented unreliability. The default is: Bronze is engineering-internal; Gold is BI-facing.

followups.q: Where do streaming CDC records from Debezium land in a Medallion architecture?
followups.a: CDC records (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE op-codes from Debezium) land in Bronze as append-only events, preserving the full change log. Silver processes them by applying the op-codes to reconstruct current state (MERGE/upsert for Type 1 attributes, new-row insert for Type 2 dimension versioning). This makes Silver an eventually consistent, auditable view of source state. Gold consumes the conformed Silver dimension and fact tables. The CDC log in Bronze is the audit trail; Silver is the interpreted state; Gold is the aggregated consumption layer.

followups.q: How do you version Medallion-layer schemas to prevent breaking downstream consumers?
followups.a: Apply schema registry (Avro/Protobuf with Confluent Schema Registry for streaming, or dbt model contracts for batch) at each layer boundary. Bronze schemas evolve freely — sources change. Silver schemas are contracted: new columns are additive (safe), column renames or drops require a migration with backward-compatible transition period. Gold schemas carry explicit model contracts (dbt `contract: enforced`) so downstream BI tools and ML pipelines get a breaking-change signal before a deployment, not a runtime failure.

q: How do you model a financial general-ledger (GL) fact table? What is the grain, what are the key dimensions, and how do you handle the debit/credit balance constraint?
a: The grain is one row per GL journal entry line item — one posting per account per document per period. Key dimensions are dim_gl_account (account number, type, financial statement line), dim_cost_center, dim_profit_center, dim_date, dim_company_code, and dim_currency. The debit/credit balance constraint is handled by storing signed amounts: positive for debit postings, negative for credit postings (or vice versa, consistently). SUM(amount) within one balanced document should equal zero; a monitoring assertion that checks SUM(amount) GROUP BY document_number HAVING ABS(SUM(amount)) > 0.01 detects unbalanced postings (rounding, missing lines, or pipeline errors). Non-additive balances (account balance at period-end) are handled by a separate periodic snapshot fact — not by summing the journal fact — because journal amounts are additive (cumulative flows), not states.
fj: I'd store debit and credit as separate columns with positive values in both.
fs: Separate debit/credit columns make SUM-based balance assertions impossible. Signed amounts (positive debit, negative credit) let you validate double-entry balance with SUM(amount) = 0 per document and support net revenue/cost queries natively.

followups.q: How do you handle foreign currency transactions in a GL fact table?
followups.a: Store both the transaction currency amount (amount_txn_ccy) and the reporting currency amount (amount_rpt_ccy) as separate fact columns, plus the exchange rate applied (fx_rate) and the dim_currency FK for the transaction currency. Reporting currency enables consistent aggregation across currencies; transaction currency preserves audit accuracy. For multi-currency roll-ups always sum amount_rpt_ccy, not amount_txn_ccy. Store the fx_rate as a non-additive fact — derive effective rates at query time from SUM(amount_rpt_ccy) / SUM(amount_txn_ccy).

followups.q: How do you handle period-close adjusting entries that post in a prior period?
followups.a: Stamp the fact with the GL posting date (accounting_date, which is the prior period) and the pipeline load date separately. The prior-period adjustment appears in the correct accounting period in reports (accounting_date determines period attribution), but you can audit when it was loaded via load_ts. The periodic snapshot fact for that prior period must be marked as restated — add a `is_restated` flag or version the snapshot. Issue an alert or SLA breach notification when adjustments post more than N days after period close.

followups.q: What's the difference between the GL journal entry fact and an account balance fact, and why do you need both?
followups.a: The journal entry fact records individual debit/credit flows (additive, high cardinality, good for drill-down and audit). The account balance fact is a periodic snapshot: one row per account per period-end, capturing the closing balance. Balance is semi-additive — valid to sum across accounts, not across time periods. You need both because 'what was the bank balance at December 31' requires the snapshot; 'what transactions drove the balance change' requires the journal. A closing balance can't be reliably derived from summing journals across time without extraordinary care.

q: How does normalization vs. denormalization tradeoff change specifically for columnar warehouses like Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks compared to row-store databases?
a: In a row-store (PostgreSQL, MySQL), normalization reduces I/O by avoiding repeated column values on disk, and joins between normalized tables are efficient with B-tree indexes. Denormalization in a row-store is purely a read-vs-write tradeoff. In a columnar warehouse, the storage argument for normalization largely disappears: dictionary encoding compresses repeated segment or country values to near-constant size regardless of repetition, and columnar pruning reads only queried columns regardless of table width. The remaining arguments for star schema normalization in columnar systems are: (1) update isolation — dimension updates touch one small dimension table, not millions of fact rows; (2) schema flexibility — adding a dimension attribute doesn't require rebuilding the fact; (3) query composability — different consumers can join different dimensions without an OBT per use case. Denormalization (OBT) is justified in columnar when one dominant query pattern must minimize latency and the schema is stable, but the storage and I/O arguments that drove normalization in row-stores are largely obsolete in the columnar era.
fj: Columnar warehouses are so fast you don't need normalization — just make everything one big flat table.
fs: Columnar engines eliminate the storage argument for normalization but not the update-isolation and schema-flexibility arguments. Star schema stays the right default; OBT is a targeted optimization for one dominant query, not a general replacement.

followups.q: How does clustering/Z-ordering in Databricks Delta Lake affect the normalization vs denormalization decision?
followups.a: Z-ordering clusters related rows together on disk, dramatically reducing I/O for range-filter queries on the Z-ordered columns. For an OBT, you can Z-order on the most common filter columns (date + customer_segment) and get near-partition-level performance. For a star schema, Z-order the fact on date_key and rely on the small dimension tables being fully cached. Z-ordering makes both patterns faster, but it doesn't eliminate the star schema's update-isolation and schema-flexibility advantages — it just means the OBT's scan penalty is lower.

followups.q: BigQuery uses nested RECORD columns. Does that change the normalization decision?
followups.a: Yes — BigQuery's native ARRAY/STRUCT (nested/repeated) support is a third option: you can embed dimension attributes as a nested RECORD inside the fact row rather than joining to a separate table or flattening to an OBT. A sales fact with a nested `customer` struct avoids a join while preserving some schema modularity. The tradeoff: querying nested fields requires UNNEST() which can be clunky; schema changes inside the struct are harder than adding a column to a separate dimension. Useful for low-cardinality one-to-one embeddings (order metadata), not for high-cardinality attributes that change independently.

followups.q: How do Snowflake's automatic clustering and micro-partition pruning interact with a star schema join?
followups.a: Snowflake uses micro-partitions (50–500MB immutable columnar chunks) with automatic clustering on the natural sort order of data load. When you join a fact (which may be clustered on date) to a small dimension, Snowflake can broadcast the dimension to all nodes and probe locally — the join is a broadcast hash join, which is fast for small dimensions. Automatic clustering on the join key (date_key, customer_key) further reduces micro-partitions scanned. For very wide star schemas with many dimension joins, Snowflake's query optimizer may push predicates from dimension filters into the fact scan (partition pruning via join filter pushdown), making the star schema pattern efficient without OBT flattening.

q: How do you use the Kimball bus matrix to plan a multi-source warehouse, and what does a cell with an 'X' actually commit you to technically?
a: The bus matrix is a planning grid: rows are business processes (Salesforce opportunities, SAP sales orders, ERP goods movements, web clickstream), columns are conformed dimensions (dim_date, dim_customer, dim_product, dim_geography, dim_sales_channel). An X in a cell commits you technically to: (1) building that dimension as a shared physical (or guaranteed-identical replicated) table with a single surrogate key space across all processes that reference it; (2) ensuring the attribute names, hierarchy levels, and rollup definitions are identical — 'customer region' means the same thing in the CRM fact and the ERP fact; (3) making drill-across reporting valid — you can join the pre-aggregated results from two facts on that dimension's attributes and the numbers will be comparable. Without the bus matrix, teams build separate dim_customers per mart that drift in definition, making drill-across silently wrong. Fill the matrix before you write a line of SQL.
fj: The bus matrix is a documentation artifact — just build the tables and document later.
fs: The bus matrix is a design contract made before coding. Each X commits you to a shared surrogate key space, identical attribute definitions, and valid drill-across — the engineering decisions that make dimensional modeling work at scale.

followups.q: What happens if two teams build separate dim_customers without a bus matrix agreement?
followups.a: They produce two customer dimensions with different surrogate keys, different segment labels, different hierarchy rollups, and potentially different entity resolution (one might merge duplicates the other doesn't). Drill-across between their fact tables produces wrong or incomparable numbers because the 'customer region' values don't match. Reconciling after the fact requires a retroactive entity-resolution project that touches both dimension tables and may require fact backfills — much more expensive than agreeing on conformance up front.

followups.q: How do you handle a dimension that exists in one process but not another on the bus matrix?
followups.a: No X in that cell — that process simply doesn't have that dimension. You cannot drill across on that dimension between processes that have it and processes that don't. For example, only the web clickstream fact has dim_device; the ERP sales fact does not. You can still analyze clickstream by device independently, but you can't compare ERP revenue vs clickstream sessions by device in one drill-across query. Document the absence explicitly; don't force an artificial dimension join where no natural relationship exists.

followups.q: How do you evolve the bus matrix when a new source system is added?
followups.a: Add the new process as a new row. Assess each existing conformed dimension column: can the new source map to it? If yes, your entity-resolution and ETL scope expands but no new dimension is needed. If no match exists, add a new column for the new dimension. Any new X creates engineering work: the conformed dimension must include the new source's entities with consistent surrogate keys. The bus matrix evolves incrementally — treat it as a living contract reviewed at each sprint before new fact tables are designed.

q: How do you model a clickstream / event-stream fact table? What are the grain, dimension, and schema challenges compared to a traditional sales fact?
a: The grain of a clickstream fact is one row per user event (page view, click, session start, conversion) — extremely high cardinality and velocity. The primary challenges vs a sales fact are: (1) sparse dimensions — not every event has a customer_key (anonymous pre-login sessions use a cookie_id as a degenerate dimension); (2) session boundary ambiguity — a session key is often computed by windowing events by inactivity timeout, making it a derived degenerate dimension; (3) schema evolution — new event types add new properties, requiring either a wide sparse table (many NULLs), a JSON/VARIANT column for event-specific properties, or an event-type-specific table per event; (4) scale — billions of rows/day requires partitioning on event_date and clustering on user_id or session_id. Model the primary fact at event grain; derive a session-level periodic snapshot for session-level metrics; and use a factless fact table pattern for conversion attribution (session_key + conversion_key + campaign_key with no measure).
fj: I'd store all clickstream in a single wide table with one column per possible event property.
fs: A single wide sparse table drowns in NULLs and can't handle schema evolution as new event types emerge. Model at event grain with a JSON/VARIANT column for event-specific props, derive session snapshots, and use factless facts for conversion attribution.

code.label: event fact + session snapshot
lines: -- event grain: one row per event
-- session snapshot: one row per session

followups.q: How do you stitch anonymous pre-login events to a logged-in customer in the fact table?
followups.a: At login, the application should emit an identity-stitching event that maps the anonymous cookie_id to the authenticated customer_id. In the warehouse, a silver-layer identity map table records cookie_id → customer_key resolutions with a resolved_ts. A backfill job retroactively stamps the customer_key on pre-login events for that cookie_id within a lookback window. Post-backfill, the event fact has a customer_key for the stitched portion; unresolved pre-login events retain NULL customer_key and are counted as anonymous sessions.

followups.q: How do you define and compute session_id in a warehouse if the application doesn't emit it?
followups.a: Use a session-boundary window function: partition events by user_id (or cookie_id), order by event_ts, and flag a new session when the gap from the previous event exceeds a threshold (typically 30 minutes). Generate a session_id using MD5(user_id || session_start_ts). This is computed in a silver model. The challenge: late-arriving events can cross a session boundary, requiring the session definition to be re-run retroactively — use a configurable lookback window and make the session model idempotent.

followups.q: How do you attribute a conversion to a marketing campaign touchpoint when there are multiple touchpoints per session?
followups.a: Attribution is a modeling decision, not a schema decision. Store all touchpoints in the event fact (campaign_key on each event). Build a separate attribution model fact or bridge: one row per (conversion_key, touchpoint_event_key, attribution_weight). The weight depends on the chosen model — last-touch (1.0 on the last touchpoint), first-touch (1.0 on first), linear (1/N equally distributed), or data-driven (ML-derived). The factless fact stores all touchpoints; the attribution bridge applies the weight model and can be rebuilt when the model changes without touching the raw event fact.

q: What is Dimensional Modeling's concept of a 'grain declaration' and how does violating grain create a 'mixed-grain' fact table that silently corrupts analytics?
a: Declaring the grain is step two of Kimball's four-step dimensional design process: you state, in precise business terms, exactly what one row in the fact table represents — 'one row per customer order line item on a sales order'. Every column in the fact must be consistent with that grain: a measure must be additive at that grain (line revenue is fine; total_order_value is not, because it repeats on every line). A mixed-grain fact table violates this by embedding measures or dimension attributes from two different grains in one table — for example, storing both line_revenue (line grain) and order_discount (order grain, which repeats on every line). When an analyst SUMs order_discount they multiply it by the number of lines per order — a silent inflation that produces plausible-looking but wrong totals. The diagnosis test: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fct WHERE [grain_columns] GROUP BY [grain_columns] HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 — duplicates mean the grain is wrong or the ETL is introducing fan-out.
fj: I'd just declare the grain as 'one row per sale' and add whatever columns seem useful.
fs: The grain must be stated precisely ('one row per order line item'), and every column validated against it. A coarser-grain measure that repeats across lines (order_discount) will be multiplied by line count when summed — a silent, plausible-looking inflation with no error message.

code.label: detect grain violation
lines: -- any row = mixed grain or fan-out

followups.q: You discover that fct_order_lines has been mixing order-level and line-level measures for two years. How do you fix it safely?
followups.a: First, scope the blast radius: which BI dashboards and downstream models use the corrupted measures? Audit queries in your BI tool's metadata. Second, extract the order-grain measures into a separate fct_orders table at order grain. Third, remove the repeated order-grain columns from fct_order_lines. Fourth, update downstream models to join the two facts via the order degenerate dimension. Fifth, re-run historical validation: compare pre- and post-fix totals for known report periods to quantify the inflation and issue a data quality incident notice. Do not silently fix — the inflation affected two years of reports.

followups.q: How does dbt help enforce grain at development time?
followups.a: dbt's `dbt_utils.unique_combination_of_columns` test asserts that the combination of declared grain columns is unique in the model. Run it in CI so any ETL change that introduces grain violations fails the pipeline before deployment. Also use `dbt_utils.expression_is_true` to assert that a repeated measure (like order_discount) is identical across all lines of the same order — a non-zero variance signals a fan-out or incorrect value.

followups.q: What is 'grain aggregation' and when is it useful without violating grain?
followups.a: Grain aggregation means rolling a fine-grain fact up to a coarser grain in a separate model — e.g. materializing fct_order_lines (line grain) into fct_orders_daily (customer + date grain) as a pre-aggregated mart for dashboards that always report at that level. This is not a mixed-grain violation — each model has its own declared, consistent grain. The anti-pattern is mixing grains in one table; pre-aggregated derivative tables at a coarser grain are a valid read optimization.

q: How does the Snowflake Schema differ from a Star Schema in the treatment of dimension hierarchies, and when does the join overhead in a Snowflake Schema actually outweigh its storage savings on a modern columnar warehouse?
a: In a Star Schema every dimension is fully denormalized into one flat table—dim_product holds category, subcategory, and department as redundant columns. A Snowflake Schema normalizes those into sub-dimension tables: dim_product → dim_subcategory → dim_category, each joined by surrogate keys. Storage savings come from eliminating repeated string values for high-cardinality parents; in a Snowflake Database or BigQuery the columnar encoding already compresses those repetitions to near-zero, so the storage argument largely disappears. The remaining use case for the Snowflake Schema is a many-level ragged hierarchy you must navigate dynamically (org charts, bill-of-materials), where the normalized parent-child table beats a wide denormalized one. For typical BI workloads the extra joins hurt query plan simplicity and BI-tool compatibility without a measurable storage win, so the Star Schema remains the default in all modern columnar platforms.
fj: Snowflake Schema is better because normalization is always correct practice.
fs: Columnar compression already handles repetition. Snowflake Schema adds join complexity without storage benefits on modern platforms. Only use it for genuinely deep dynamic hierarchies.

followups.q: Your Snowflake Schema has dim_product → dim_subcategory → dim_category. A BI analyst reports that their Tableau queries are running a three-way join. How do you fix this without scrapping the normalized model?
followups.a: Materialize a flattened dim_product_flat view that pre-joins the three tables, exposing all hierarchy attributes in one wide virtual or physical table. Tableau (and most BI tools) connect to the flat view; the normalized tables remain the authoritative source. Use dbt to maintain both—dim_subcategory and dim_category as building blocks, dim_product_flat as a downstream mart model. The Snowflake Schema's normalization is preserved as a source-of-truth layer; the flat view is a read-optimized derivative.

followups.q: Snowflake Schema vs. Star Schema on a Snowflake Database—is there a naming coincidence worth clarifying in interviews?
followups.a: Yes—always clarify. The Snowflake Schema is a dimensional modeling pattern (normalized sub-dimension tables) invented by Ralph Kimball-era practitioners. Snowflake (the database) is a cloud data warehouse product. They share a name but are unrelated. Snowflake the database fully supports Star Schemas and is the more common setup there; the Snowflake Schema pattern is rarely the right default on Snowflake the platform because its columnar compression negates the storage motivation.

q: You have a Data Vault 2.0 integration layer and need to publish a gold-layer Dimensional Modeling mart for Snowflake-based BI. Walk through how a hub, link, and satellite map to star schema components.
a: In Data Vault 2.0, a hub holds the business key and hash key for an entity—hub_customer becomes the seed for dim_customer. A satellite hanging off that hub holds the slowly changing descriptive attributes (name, tier, address) in append-only rows with load timestamps; the latest satellite row per hub key is your current dimension attributes. A link between hub_customer and hub_product becomes the grain of a transaction fact—its hash key is your fact table's grain key, with measures joining from measure satellites (sat_order_amounts). The dimensional modeling mart layer materializes these into a proper Star Schema: one dim_customer built from hub_customer + current sat_customer row, one fct_orders built from link_order + sat_order_amounts. Data Vault is the integration layer; the Star Schema is the consumption layer. The Medallion Architecture terminology maps Bronze → raw vaults, Silver → business vault, Gold → dimensional marts.
fj: Data Vault replaces the star schema—just query the hubs and satellites directly.
fs: Querying Data Vault directly requires joining dozens of tables per business question. The Star Schema mart is a mandatory consumption layer built on top of Data Vault—not optional.

code.label: satellite → dim column
lines: -- latest sat row per hub key
ORDER BY load_ts DESC LIMIT 1) s

followups.q: What is a Business Vault in Data Vault 2.0, and how does it differ from the Raw Vault?
followups.a: The Raw Vault loads source data as-is with no business rules—hubs, links, and satellites mirror the source exactly, insert-only. The Business Vault adds computed satellites (sat_customer_classification) and derived links that apply business logic—like computing a customer tier from raw income and score, or resolving duplicate hubs across sources. Business Vault changes don't touch the Raw Vault, so all source data remains auditable. The Medallion Architecture analogy: Raw Vault = Bronze/Silver boundary, Business Vault = Silver, dimensional marts = Gold.

followups.q: How do you handle a source system that sends full snapshots (not deltas) into your Data Vault satellite?
followups.a: Use the hashdiff column: on each load compute hash(all tracked columns) per business key, compare to the latest stored hashdiff in the satellite. Insert only rows where the hashdiff differs. This makes full-snapshot loading idempotent—rows with unchanged attributes generate no new satellite rows, so the insert-only guarantee holds even when the source sends 100% of records every time.

q: Explain the Medallion Architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold) in a Lakehouse context. What data quality guarantees does each layer enforce, and how does it relate to Dimensional Modeling and Data Vault?
a: The Medallion Architecture is a multi-hop data quality pattern common in Databricks Delta Lake and similar Lakehouse platforms. Bronze is raw ingestion: full-fidelity records with no transformations, schema as-landed, append-only or upserted by primary key. It maps to the Data Vault Raw Vault concept—auditability and replayability are the priorities. Silver is cleansed and conformed: nulls handled, types cast, duplicate records resolved, business keys aligned across sources; this is where conformed dimensions and standardized column names are enforced. It maps to a Data Vault Business Vault or Kimball staging/integration layer. Gold is the presentation layer: aggregated marts, Dimensional Modeling star schemas, OBTs for BI, and feature tables for ML. The Medallion Architecture is implementation-framework-agnostic—you can run it on Delta Lake, Iceberg, or Parquet—while Data Vault and Dimensional Modeling are logical design patterns that slot into Silver and Gold respectively.
fj: Medallion just means landing data in three folders: raw, clean, and reporting.
fs: Medallion is a data quality contract: Bronze = full-fidelity source, Silver = conformed and cleansed, Gold = consumption-ready marts. Each layer has defined quality guarantees and you never query Bronze for production BI.

followups.q: In a Medallion Architecture, which layer do you run dbt models against, and which layer do you run a streaming ingestion job against?
followups.a: Streaming ingestion writes to Bronze (raw capture, minimal transformation, append/upsert). dbt models run from Silver onward: dbt staging models clean Bronze to Silver, dbt intermediate/mart models build Silver to Gold. Running dbt directly against Bronze is an anti-pattern because Bronze data is intentionally unclean and dbt models would need to defensively handle every source quirk instead of standardized inputs.

followups.q: How do you enforce schema evolution in a Medallion Bronze layer on Delta Lake?
followups.a: Set `mergeSchema = true` on Delta writes so new source columns are automatically added to the Bronze table without breaking the pipeline. Pair this with schema-on-read patterns in Silver—Silver dbt models explicitly SELECT named columns, so new Bronze columns are ignored until a Silver model opts into them. Alert on schema changes via Delta's DESCRIBE HISTORY or event hooks so the Silver maintainer knows to review new columns before promoting them.

q: You are modeling Salesforce CRM data into a Silver Medallion layer and then a Dimensional Modeling gold mart. Walk through the key entities, the modeling decisions for polymorphic relationships, and how you handle Salesforce's object model quirks.
a: Core Salesforce entities map to warehouse objects as follows: Account → dim_account (companies, conformed with ERP customer master), Contact → dim_contact (individuals at accounts, many-to-one with dim_account), Opportunity → fct_opportunity (grain: one row per opportunity stage snapshot or one row per opportunity close event), OpportunityLineItem → fct_opportunity_line (grain: one row per product per opportunity). The key modeling challenge is Salesforce's polymorphic lookup: the WhoId and WhatId fields on Activity (Task/Event) can reference any object type—Contact, Lead, Account, or custom objects. Model this with a bridge table (bridge_activity_entity) holding activity_key, entity_type, and entity_key, or split into type-specific fact tables. SCD Type 2 is critical for Opportunity Stage history (stage_history in Salesforce maps to versioned dimension rows or an accumulating snapshot). Lead-to-Account-to-Opportunity conversion tracking requires an accumulating snapshot fact with conversion milestone dates. Conformed dimensions (dim_date, dim_owner/sales_rep) shared with ERP facts enable Salesforce-ERP drill-across for revenue attribution.
fj: I'd just land all Salesforce objects as flat tables and let analysts join them.
fs: Salesforce's polymorphic lookups and object relationships require deliberate modeling—bridge tables for WhoId/WhatId, accumulating snapshots for lead-to-close funnels, and SCD Type 2 for opportunity stage history. Flat landing breaks reporting.

followups.q: How do you sync Salesforce CRM data incrementally into a Bronze Medallion layer? What API limitations affect your design?
followups.a: Use Salesforce Bulk API 2.0 with query-all plus LastModifiedDate filters for initial and incremental loads. Key limitation: SystemModstamp changes on parent record updates but not always child record updates—polling only on LastModifiedDate misses some changes. Mitigate with daily full-refresh of small high-change objects and incremental on large stable ones. Salesforce's API rate limits (concurrent Bulk API jobs, daily API call volume) constrain parallelism—design your ingestion to batch multiple objects per job within rate limits.

followups.q: Salesforce uses 18-character case-insensitive IDs as natural keys. How do you handle these in a Data Vault or dimensional warehouse?
followups.a: Use the 15-character ID as the canonical business key (it's the unique identifier; the 18-character version adds checksum characters for case-insensitive contexts). Normalize to lowercase before hashing for Data Vault hub keys or generating dbt surrogate keys—this prevents hash collisions between 15- and 18-character versions of the same ID that arrive from different API endpoints. Always store the original source ID alongside the hash key for auditability.

q: How do you model SAP ERP source data—specifically the SD (Sales Distribution), MM (Materials Management), and FI (Financial Accounting) modules—into a Medallion Silver layer and then a Dimensional Modeling gold mart?
a: SAP SD maps to sales facts: VBAK (sales order header) and VBAP (line item) → fct_sales_orders at line-item grain; VBRK/VBRP (billing) → fct_invoices. SAP MM maps to procurement: EKKO/EKPO (purchase order header/line) → fct_purchase_orders; MSEG (material document) → fct_goods_movements. SAP FI maps to the GL: BKPF/BSEG (accounting document header/line) → fct_gl_postings at posting-line grain. Conformed dimensions across modules: dim_material (from MM's MARA/MARC), dim_vendor (LFA1), dim_customer (KNA1—conformed with Salesforce CRM dim_account for full order-to-cash visibility), dim_cost_center (CSKS/CSKT), dim_date. Key modeling challenge: SAP uses 'document keys' (MANDT, BUKRS, GJAHR, BELNR) as compound natural keys—hash them together into a surrogate or Data Vault hub key. Posting periods and company codes require a fiscal calendar dimension specific to each SAP client, not the standard Gregorian calendar. Currency handling: SAP stores amounts in document currency and local currency; always carry both in the fact with a dim_currency and an exchange-rate satellite for multi-currency Dimensional Modeling.
fj: SAP data is too complex to model—just expose SAP reports directly to analysts.
fs: SAP's multi-table document model maps cleanly to dimensional facts at the right grain. The key complexity is compound document keys, fiscal calendars per company code, and multi-currency amounts—all solvable with deliberate Dimensional Modeling.

lines: -- hash compound doc key to surrogate

followups.q: SAP BSEG (GL line items) is one of the largest tables in an SAP system—often billions of rows. How does this affect your Medallion Bronze ingestion strategy?
followups.a: BSEG has no single reliable incremental key—SAP doesn't set a standard updated_at. Use CPUDT (posting date) plus CPUTM (posting time) for incremental loads, supplementing with a daily full-load of the current open fiscal period (postings can be reversed and corrected within the period). Partition the Bronze table by posting date (BUDAT) so period-level full refreshes don't require full-table scans. For the initial load, use SAP Operational Data Provisioning (ODP) or direct ABAP-level table extraction via CDS views to avoid lock contention on production BSEG.

followups.q: How do you handle SAP multi-currency in a dimensional gold mart when stakeholders want all amounts in USD?
followups.a: Store three amount columns in the fact: amount_doc_currency (transaction currency), amount_local_currency (company code currency), amount_group_currency (group reporting currency, from BSEG.KURSF exchange rate). Build a separate dim_exchange_rate (or satellite in Data Vault) with daily rates per currency pair. The Gold mart materializes converted amounts using the rate current at posting_date—never at load time—so restated rates don't corrupt historical facts. Analysts select the currency column appropriate for their report; the mart never pre-converts to a single currency.

q: A new data source is a SaaS CRM (not Salesforce) and a legacy ERP (not SAP). How do you design a Silver Medallion conformed dimension for 'customer' that reconciles both sources when they have overlapping but non-identical customer records?
a: This is the entity resolution problem in the Silver Medallion layer. Step one: ingest both sources into Bronze with their native keys. Step two: in Silver, build a resolution model that matches customers across sources using deterministic rules (email exact match, phone normalized match) and probabilistic rules (name + zip similarity). The output is a mapping table: source_system, source_customer_id → warehouse_customer_id (the conformed surrogate). Step three: dim_customer in Gold uses the warehouse_customer_id as surrogate key, carries both source IDs as natural keys (crm_customer_id, erp_customer_id), and takes attributes preferentially from the source of record (CRM for contact info, ERP for billing info). All facts from both source systems stamp the warehouse_customer_id so Salesforce-ERP drill-across works across a conformed dimension. In Data Vault, this is a same-as link: a special link connecting duplicate hub entries that the resolution process identifies as the same real-world entity.
fj: I'd build separate dim_crm_customer and dim_erp_customer tables and let analysts pick.
fs: Separate unresolved dimensions break cross-source reporting. The Silver layer must resolve entity identity into a single conformed surrogate so Gold mart facts from both sources join to the same customer row.

followups.q: What is a same-as link in Data Vault 2.0 and when do you need one?
followups.a: A same-as link (SAL) is a special Data Vault link table that records the identity assertion: hub_customer row A and hub_customer row B represent the same real-world entity. It's separate from a business link (which records a relationship like order-to-customer). The SAL is created by the entity resolution process in the Business Vault and carries a confidence score and source of the match. Downstream dimensional marts use the SAL to pick a single canonical hub key for the conformed dimension surrogate.

followups.q: How do you handle the case where the entity resolution model later decides two previously-separate customers should be merged, and historical facts already exist for both?
followups.a: This is a retroactive merge. In the Data Vault model you add a new same-as link row—the Raw Vault is immutable. In the dimensional mart you have two options: re-stamp all historical facts to the new canonical surrogate (expensive, requires a full backfill), or carry an is_merged flag and a canonical_customer_key on dim_customer so queries can group on the canonical key without fact updates. Document the merge event with an effective date so pre-merge history can still be segmented.

q: Walk through how Dimensional Modeling and the Medallion Architecture handle schema evolution differently, and why this matters when your Salesforce CRM or SAP ERP source adds a new field.
a: In a Medallion Architecture Bronze layer, schema evolution is absorbed automatically via schema merge (Delta Lake mergeSchema) or Iceberg schema evolution—the new field arrives as a new column in the Bronze table without pipeline failure. Silver models selectively pick it up when a human reviews and promotes it. In Dimensional Modeling, adding a new attribute to a dimension is relatively safe (ALTER TABLE dim_customer ADD COLUMN new_field) but adding a new measure to a fact requires evaluating whether it's additive at the existing grain and backfilling historical NULL values. The real pain is SCD Type 2 columns: adding a new Type 2 column to an existing dim_customer means you have no historical versions of that attribute for records created before the column existed—those rows get NULL effective_from logic for the new attribute. In a Data Vault satellite, a new attribute simply arrives in a new satellite (or a new satellite version column) without touching existing rows—this is why Data Vault handles source-schema agility better than a pre-designed Dimensional Modeling fact or dimension. For Salesforce CRM, new custom fields are common; for SAP ERP, new custom ZZ fields per project are universal—design your Silver pipeline to ingest all columns and promote selectively to Gold.
fj: Schema changes are the DBA's problem—just alter the table and reload.
fs: Schema evolution strategy is a first-class design decision. Medallion Bronze absorbs new fields automatically; Data Vault satellites isolate new attributes; Dimensional Modeling dimensions require deliberate promotion and SCD handling for new Type 2 attributes.

followups.q: A Salesforce admin adds 20 new custom fields to the Opportunity object. How do you decide which to promote to the Gold dimensional mart?
followups.a: Evaluate each field on three criteria: business owner has named a reporting use case, the field is consistently populated above a threshold (e.g. >30% non-null on closed-won opportunities), and the field's data quality passes Silver validation rules. Fields meeting all three are added to the Silver opportunity model and promoted to dim_opportunity_attributes or fct_opportunity depending on whether they're descriptive or additive. The remaining 15 fields stay in Silver as available columns, documented in the data catalog, but not in the Gold mart until a business case exists. This prevents Gold mart column sprawl.

followups.q: How does Delta Lake's OPTIMIZE and Z-ORDER interact with a Medallion Gold dimensional mart?
followups.a: OPTIMIZE compacts small Delta files from streaming or incremental writes into larger Parquet files, improving scan performance. Z-ORDER on the most common filter columns (e.g. Z-ORDER BY (customer_key, date_key) on a fct_orders) co-locates related rows in the same files, so queries filtering on those columns skip more files via Delta's file statistics. Run OPTIMIZE + Z-ORDER on Gold tables after each batch load cycle. For Silver tables with high write frequency, schedule OPTIMIZE less aggressively to avoid I/O contention during business hours.

q: Compare Data Vault 2.0 hash keys (MD5 vs SHA-256) on collision probability, key width, and index performance. When would you choose one over the other for a high-volume enterprise warehouse?
a: MD5 produces 128-bit (16-byte) hashes with a practical collision probability of ~1 in 2^64 under the birthday paradox—negligible for entity volumes up to trillions of rows. SHA-256 produces 256-bit (32-byte) hashes with collision probability ~1 in 2^128—effectively zero for any realistic warehouse. Data Vault 2.0 (DV2.0 as standardized by Dan Linstedt post-2013) recommends SHA-256 as the standard after MD5 collisions became a theoretical concern for cryptographic contexts; for a data warehouse hub with millions to billions of rows, MD5 is still safe in practice but SHA-256 is the defensible standard. Key width trade-off: SHA-256 keys are twice as wide as MD5, increasing index size and join I/O on every hub-satellite-link join. On Snowflake Database and BigQuery, BINARY(16)/BINARY(32) is more compact than VARCHAR(32)/VARCHAR(64) hex strings—store as BYTES/BINARY, not hex strings. For Medallion Bronze/Silver layers at multi-TB scale, prefer MD5 for smaller index footprint if the data is not security-sensitive; use SHA-256 for regulatory/compliance contexts where key integrity must be auditable.
fj: Just use MD5 everywhere—collision risk is theoretical.
fs: DV2.0 standardizes SHA-256 for auditability and future-proofing. MD5 is acceptable for non-compliance workloads where index size matters. Store as BINARY not VARCHAR to halve key width.

followups.q: Two source systems send the same customer with business key 'CUST-001'. How does a Data Vault hub handle multi-source loads without creating duplicate hub rows?
followups.a: The hub's natural key is the business key value ('CUST-001'), not the source system. The hash key is hash(business_key_value) — identical from both sources. The hub has a UNIQUE constraint on the hash key, so a duplicate insert is a no-op (or caught by MERGE). The record_source column on the hub row tracks which source first established the key; subsequent sources don't overwrite it. Multiple satellites (sat_customer_crm, sat_customer_erp) hang off the same hub key, each loaded by its source, preserving source-system attributes separately without collision.

followups.q: How do you validate that your Data Vault hash key function is deterministic across Snowflake, Databricks, and any Python-based ingestion tools?
followups.a: Standardize the hash function inputs: (1) uppercase the business key before hashing, (2) trim whitespace, (3) use a consistent NULL placeholder string ('~' is the DV2.0 convention) for any NULL components of composite keys, (4) use a consistent delimiter ('^' is common) between composite key parts. Test with a cross-platform fixture: compute hash('CUST-001') in SQL on each platform and in Python hashlib, and assert equality. Document the exact specification in a team data vault standards doc so any new tool can be validated on day one.

q: How does the Medallion Architecture handle late-arriving data and out-of-order events differently from a traditional Dimensional Modeling nightly-batch ETL?
a: In a nightly-batch Dimensional Modeling ETL, late-arriving facts use the 'unknown member' placeholder (surrogate key -1) with a scheduled backfill job; the pipeline window is defined and late data beyond the window may be dropped or require a manual reprocess. In a Medallion Architecture on a streaming Lakehouse, Bronze absorbs events at any time via Structured Streaming with event-time watermarks; late events within the watermark window (e.g., 24 hours) are merged into the correct Bronze partition automatically via Delta Lake's MERGE INTO with a time-range predicate. Silver models run as triggered incremental refreshes after Bronze batch windows, catching late Bronze rows naturally. Gold models downstream of Silver use dbt incremental models with a `unique_key` that re-processes any changed Silver rows. The key advantage over batch SCD ETL: a late-arriving CRM event doesn't require manual pipeline intervention—it flows through Bronze → Silver → Gold in the next refresh cycle. SCD Type 2 in Gold still requires care: a late-arriving dimension change generates a new version row and may require re-stamping recently loaded facts if their surrogate key was frozen before the dimension change arrived.
fj: Late data is an edge case—just rerun the pipeline and it'll fix itself.
fs: Late data in a Medallion streaming Bronze is absorbed within the watermark window automatically. The challenge is SCD Type 2 in Gold: late dimension changes require fact re-stamping, which is the same problem as traditional batch ETL but needs explicit handling in incremental dbt models.

followups.q: How do you define a watermark in Structured Streaming for a Bronze Medallion ingestion layer, and what happens to events that arrive after the watermark expires?
followups.a: A watermark is a time threshold: `withWatermark('event_time', '24 hours')` tells the streaming engine to wait up to 24 hours for late events before finalizing a time window. Events arriving within 24 hours of their event_time are merged into the correct window. Events arriving after the watermark expires are dropped (for aggregation windows) or left unjoined (for stream-stream joins). For Bronze ingestion where you want zero data loss, use a longer watermark or decouple Bronze ingestion from the aggregation—land all events in Bronze regardless of lateness, and handle late-data business logic in Silver.

followups.q: In a Medallion Silver layer, how do you deduplicate records that arrive multiple times from a Salesforce CRM bulk API re-export?
followups.a: In Delta Lake Silver: use `MERGE INTO silver_opportunity USING incoming ON silver.opportunity_id = incoming.opportunity_id AND silver.last_modified = incoming.last_modified WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...`. This is idempotent: identical re-exports generate no changes. For records where SystemModstamp changed (a real update), add `WHEN MATCHED AND silver.last_modified < incoming.last_modified THEN UPDATE`. The Silver table becomes the deduplicated single source of truth per opportunity version, and Gold dbt models run downstream without needing dedup logic of their own.

q: How do you implement SCD Type 2 correctly for a dim_product dimension that is shared as a conformed dimension across three gold-layer facts in a Medallion Architecture?
a: First, build dim_product as a Silver model (dbt snapshot or custom MERGE logic) that versions product rows with surrogate key, effective_from, effective_to, and is_current. The Silver dim_product is the single source of truth. Each of the three Gold facts (fct_sales, fct_inventory, fct_returns) stamps the product_key surrogate at event load time via a range join against Silver dim_product (event_date BETWEEN effective_from AND effective_to). Because dim_product is a conformed dimension, all three facts share identical product_key values—drill-across on product_category works because the category value for a given product_key is identical across all three facts. The failure mode to avoid: each Gold fact team independently re-creating their own product dimension logic, which produces different product_key values for the same product and breaks conformance. Governance rule: one team owns Silver dim_product; all Gold fact models are consumers.
fj: Each fact team should build their own product dimension to move faster.
fs: Independent product dimensions per fact team destroys conformance—product_key values diverge and drill-across fails silently. One Silver dim_product, consumed by all Gold facts. Conformed dimension ownership must be centralized.

followups.q: How does a conformed dimension in a Medallion Gold layer interact with a semantic layer tool like dbt MetricFlow or Cube?
followups.a: The semantic layer defines the `dim_product` table once as a shared dimension entity with its join keys and attributes. Each metric (fct_sales.revenue, fct_inventory.stock_level) references `dim_product` as a shared dimension in its metric definition. When a BI user groups revenue and stock by product_category, the semantic layer generates two separate SQL queries (one per metric) grouped by product_category, then outer-joins the results—this is the drill-across pattern enforced automatically. The semantic layer guarantees conformance at query time even if an analyst tries to write a direct fact-to-fact join.

followups.q: A product's category changes (SCD Type 2 event), creating a new dim_product row. One of the three Gold facts hasn't re-stamped its product_key yet due to a pipeline lag. How does this manifest in BI reports?
followups.a: The lagging fact still joins to the old product row (old category) while the other two facts have moved to the new surrogate (new category). A drill-across report groups all three facts by category: the lagging fact's revenue still appears under the old category while inventory and returns appear under the new one. The symptom: total revenue by category doesn't match total orders by category for the period after the change. The fix: SLA on Gold fact re-stamping after a dim_product SCD2 event, monitored by a dbt test that asserts all three fcts have no product_key values pointing to non-current dim_product rows older than the SLA window.

q: What are the three pillars of a well-designed Medallion Silver layer for ERP and CRM data: data typing, business key conformance, and referential integrity checks? How do you implement each?
a: Data typing: cast all source strings to correct SQL types (DATE, NUMERIC, BOOLEAN) at the Silver boundary rather than in Gold models. SAP ERP sends BUDAT (posting date) as CHAR(8) in YYYYMMDD format; Silver casts it to DATE. Salesforce CRM sends amounts as strings with currency codes; Silver casts to NUMERIC and carries currency separately. Business key conformance: normalize and trim business keys before hashing or joining—uppercase SAP material numbers (MATNR is always uppercase in SAP, but some extraction tools lowercase), trim Salesforce 18-character IDs to 15 characters, strip leading zeros from SAP cost centers. Referential integrity checks: implement dbt tests or Great Expectations rules that assert every Silver ERP posting (BSEG) has a matching entry in Silver GL account master (SKA1) and every Silver CRM opportunity has a matching Silver account—these are the Silver-layer equivalent of database foreign key constraints. Failures route records to a quarantine table (not dropped) with a failure_reason column, ensuring Bronze data is never silently lost and the Gold mart never has broken foreign key joins.
fj: Just land everything in Silver and let Gold models handle data quality.
